preFix.title: Salambos with kirsch cream
preFix.subtitle: French choux cigars filled with kirsch-scented cream and glazed with caramel
preFix.excerpt: Salambos are an old French patisserie form: choux pastry piped as short cigar shapes rather than rounds, filled with kirsch-flavoured whipped cream, and glazed with a thin caramel or fondant. They appear in French confectionery literature from the nineteenth century, named after Flaubert's novel.
preFix.sourceNotes: Salambos are documented in French patisserie manuals from the late nineteenth century, including works now in the public domain. The name references Flaubert's 1862 novel Salammbo; the confection appeared shortly after the novel's success. The choux-pastry-and-cream formula is a standard French patisserie technique.

content.text: Salambos are made from the same choux paste as profiteroles, piped differently. The

is a compact cigar of about 8 cm, piped with a plain 1.5 cm nozzle. Once baked and cooled, a small hole is pierced at each end and the shells are filled with kirsch-spiked whipped cream piped in under slight pressure. The caramel glaze is applied by briefly resting the top of each filled pastry on the surface of warm caramel.

items.name: Kirsch
items.prepNote: 2 tbsp; or 1 tsp vanilla extract if preferred

content.text: Make and bake the choux shells

Preheat the oven to 200 degrees C (conventional). Bring the water, butter, and salt to a rolling boil in a medium saucepan. Tip in the flour all at once and beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the paste pulls from the sides of the pan in a ball. Cook for 1 minute more, then remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Add the eggs a little at a time, beating until smooth after each addition, until the paste is glossy and falls from the spoon in a thick ribbon. Fit a piping bag with a plain 1.5 cm nozzle. Pipe 8 cm cigars onto lined baking trays, spacing them 5 cm apart. Bake for 20 minutes, then reduce to 170 degrees C (conventional) and bake for a further 10 to 15 minutes until deep golden and hollow-sounding when tapped. Pierce a small hole in each end of every shell with a skewer. Cool completely on a rack.

Whip the cold cream with the icing sugar to soft peaks. Stir in the kirsch. Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a small plain nozzle. Press the nozzle into the hole at one end of each shell and pipe cream in gently until the shell feels heavy and full. For the caramel glaze: combine the caster sugar and water in a small saucepan over medium heat. Stir until dissolved, then boil without stirring until the caramel turns a deep amber. Remove from the heat. Working quickly and carefully, rest the top of each filled salambo on the surface of the caramel for 2 to 3 seconds, then set aside. The caramel will harden within a minute. Serve the same day.
